original: Your personal security team has been briefed, and are dressed as a mix of common spacers, local techs, and Luddic laborers. Their arsenal is multitude and well-concealed.
Your own hand finds your trusty sidearm, a matte polycarbonate pistol. An ideal firearm for use in tight spaces, its mass rests securely in your holster.
You, of course, have your clutch of devilish little grenades built to resist milliradar detection. Fearful and unexpected, they trigger with a practiced twitch from your hand.

You, of course, have a monomolecular wire hidden in a false fingertip, ready to deploy into an invisible cutting edge with but a short, sharp twist. It is a venerable weapon favored by assassins of a certain class.

You, of course, need trust nothing more than your limbs. Starship combat is mass and energy applied at scale; absurdly reduced to the scale of the human body, the mechanics applied are not completely dissimilar.
Your hand finds the hilt of your officers' sword at your side, the mass of a steel blade a comfort shared with warriors dating to the primitive conflicts of Old Earth.

You stand with Bornanew in an uncomfortably narrow alley. The sky above sends nasty rivulets of fluid down the conduits and moldy canopies that diffuse the light into a flattening murk.

Your security team is invisible, discreet in nearby alcoves and rooftops.

$global.jethroName runs his hands over a door, as if feeling under it's moisture-warped surface for a clue.
Bornanew takes a step back then slams a powerful leg into a worn spot, about waist-high. With a sharp crack, the entire door falls inward, crumbling hinges and locking mechanism still attached.

Bornanew charges inside. You follow his steps through an entryway, plastic rain cloaks mildewing, through a prefab kitchen and wash unit stained the yellow of old teeth. A sitting area, chair toppled. There's a muffled thump from above.

Bornanew glances at you, his eyes giving a bounce upwards. Ulmus Pond.
